title: Coin Triplets (Penney's Game)

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-05-stochastic-processes.md

setup.narrative: Toss a fair coin until a target three-letter pattern appears. The chain has states corresponding to the longest suffix of the toss sequence that's a prefix of the target pattern.
rules: Part A: expected number of tosses to first see each pattern.
Part B: probability HHH appears before THH.
Part C: how should you choose your pattern in a head-to-head game (Penney's Game)?
setup.question: Find E[wait for HHH], E[wait for THH], P(HHH before THH), and Penney's optimal counter-strategy.

learningObjectives: Build the Markov chain on prefix-match states (S, H, HH, HHH for HHH).
Set up and solve the expected-time equations.
Recognize the asymmetry: expected wait for HHH (14) is much longer than for THH (8).
Understand why Penney's Game is non-transitive.

stages.label: Set up E[wait for HHH]
stages.prompt: States: S (no progress), H (last 1 was H), HH (last 2 were HH), HHH (absorbing). Expected-time equations?
stages.explanation: Each state takes 1 step to transition. From S: H sends to H, T stays at S. From H: H -> HH, T -> S. From HH: H -> HHH (absorb!), T -> S.

stages.label: Solve for E[HHH wait]
stages.prompt: From mu_HH = 1 + mu_S/2; mu_H = 1 + (mu_S + mu_HH)/2; mu_S = 1 + (mu_S + mu_H)/2. Solve.
stages.explanation: Substituting: mu_HH = 1 + mu_S/2. mu_H = 1 + (mu_S + 1 + mu_S/2)/2 = 1 + (3 mu_S/2 + 1)/2 = 1 + 3 mu_S/4 + 1/2 = 3/2 + 3 mu_S/4. mu_S = 1 + (mu_S + 3/2 + 3 mu_S/4)/2. Algebra yields mu_S = 14.

stages.label: Compare: E[wait for THH]
stages.prompt: For THH (similar setup), the expected wait is...

options.text: 8 tosses.  (THH benefits from no 'collapse on T' issue: a T from any state at most resets you to T, not to S.)

stages.explanation: Solving the analogous system for THH gives mu_S = 8. Conway's leading-number method confirms: HHH overlaps with itself at lengths 1, 2, 3 -> 2^1 + 2^2 + 2^3 = 14. THH overlaps only at length 3 -> 2^3 = 8.

stages.label: Part B: P(HHH before THH)
stages.prompt: Why is P(HHH before THH) so small?

options.text: Once any T appears, the future contains the suffix '..HH' which completes THH BEFORE HHH (since HHH requires 3 H's after the T, but THH already has the T before the H's). So HHH wins ONLY if the first three tosses are HHH directly. P = (1/2)^3 = 1/8.

stages.explanation: Once T appears, any subsequent HH completes THH. To get HHH first, you'd need three Hs BEFORE any T appears. P(first 3 tosses all H) = 1/8.

stages.label: Part C: Penney's Game (non-transitivity)
stages.prompt: If Player 1 announces HHH, Player 2's best counter is THH (winning prob 7/8). What's the surprising property of this game?

options.text: Non-transitivity: A beats B and B beats C does NOT imply A beats C. So given Player 1's pick, Player 2 can ALWAYS choose a pattern that wins with probability >= 2/3. Always go SECOND.
It's symmetric -- both players have equal chances.

stages.explanation: Penney's table: every length-3 pattern has a counter that beats it with probability >= 2/3. The optimal strategy: take the LAST TWO characters of opponent's pattern and prepend the OPPOSITE of opponent's second character.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Use Conway's method: for any pattern P, E[wait for P] = sum over k of 2^k where the prefix of length k matches a suffix of length k of P.
Prove non-transitivity by exhibiting a cycle of length-4 patterns.
What's E[wait for HHTTHH]? (Conway: prefix overlaps -> 64 + 4 + 2 = 70.)
